speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. Today we're focusing on how GitHub stops being just a pile of issues and pull requests and starts becoming an organized workspace. The big tools are labels, milestones, projects, and cross-references.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is the part where GitHub becomes less of a list and more of a system?

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. These are the organizational layer. They help people categorize work, set priorities, group related items, and connect conversations across issues and pull requests.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for our learners, especially people using screen readers, that organization matters a lot.

speaker: ALEX
text: It really does. Good organization reduces hunting. Instead of opening issue after issue, you can filter, sort, and listen for useful metadata like labels, milestone names, and project status.

speaker: ALEX
text: A quick administrative note before we teach the tools. This lesson pairs with the chapter's audio episode, and there are also related appendices, including a projects deep dive and a glossary. The authoritative references behind this lesson are GitHub Docs on labels, milestones, and projects.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there's also a workshop task in this chapter, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. This chapter's workshop recommendation is a guided triage activity. You are asked to read an issue and post a structured triage recommendation that a maintainer could act on immediately.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So not just, "I think this is important," but a usable recommendation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The challenge count is one guided challenge. There's no automation check by default. Your evidence is a structured comment in your assigned challenge issue. And the learning pattern is inspect, classify, explain.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That pattern sounds simple, but also very real-world.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. In open source, triage is often the difference between chaos and momentum.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Walk us through the actual challenge.

speaker: ALEX
text: Your goal is to read the details of a Learning Room issue and post a structured triage recommendation. You're working in your Learning Room repository on GitHub.com. You'll use your assigned Chapter 9 challenge issue plus one other open issue that you inspect. First, open the Issues tab in your repository. Second, find an open issue that doesn't already have labels, or use one assigned by a facilitator. Third, read the title and full description carefully.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And what are learners looking for while reading?

speaker: ALEX
text: A few things. What type of work is this? Documentation fix, bug report, accessibility improvement, new content, something else? How urgent does it seem? Is it blocking other work, is it nice-to-have, or is the urgency unclear? And what file or area of the repository does it affect?

speaker: JAMIE
text: So before touching labels, you're forming a mental model.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Then you open your assigned challenge issue, the one titled something like Chapter 9.1 with your name, and in the comment box you post a recommendation in a four-part format. You name the issue number. You suggest labels, usually one to three, from examples like documentation, bug, accessibility, enhancement, or good first issue. You suggest a milestone, or say none and give a reason. You suggest a project board column such as To Do, In Progress, or Needs Triage. And then you give a one-sentence reason explaining the choice.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's nicely constrained. It forces clear thinking.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. And if you happen to have write access, you can go beyond recommending and directly apply the labels and milestone on the issue itself. Then activate the Comment button to submit your recommendation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There was also an important accessibility tip here.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. If you're browsing available labels with a screen reader, open the repository's Labels page using the slash labels path. That page presents label names and descriptions together, which is often easier to review than a compact dropdown.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And when are you done?

speaker: ALEX
text: When your triage recommendation comment appears in your assigned challenge issue with all four fields filled in.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What counts as evidence for completing the chapter?

speaker: ALEX
text: That comment is your evidence. After you're done, close your Chapter 9 challenge issue. If you also applied labels directly, mention that in your comment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the expected outcomes?

speaker: ALEX
text: You should be able to read an issue and recommend appropriate labels, a milestone, and project placement. You should understand triage reasoning even if you don't have maintainer permissions. And you should be able to leave a clear note that a maintainer could act on immediately.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's also normalize getting stuck.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. If you're unsure which label to choose, start with one basic label like documentation, bug, or accessibility. If the milestone is unclear, writing none with a reason is valid triage. If you don't know the project board placement, Needs Triage is the correct default. If the issue itself is unclear, say that. "Issue description is unclear, needs more detail" is useful triage feedback. And you can always ask a facilitator to review your one-sentence reason before you post.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I love the chapter's core idea here: triage is about clarity, not authority.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. You do not need maintainer permissions to be helpful. A clear recommendation saves maintainers time and speeds collaboration. This is a daily open-source skill.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for continued learning, there was a GitHub Skills course mentioned.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The Introduction to Repository Management course is a good follow-up for labels, milestones, and contributor settings.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's move into labels. What are they, in plain language?

speaker: ALEX
text: Labels are colored tags applied to issues and pull requests. They communicate category, priority, or status at a glance.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And from an audio or screen reader perspective?

speaker: ALEX
text: When you scan an issue list, labels are announced alongside the issue title. A screen reader might say, "Label: bug," or "Labels: accessibility, good first issue." That makes labels more than visual decoration. They're spoken metadata.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What are the standard labels learners will commonly encounter?

speaker: ALEX
text: A typical set includes bug for something not working as expected, enhancement for a new feature or improvement, documentation for docs-only work, good first issue for beginner-friendly tasks, help wanted when maintainers want community help, question when more information is needed, invalid when an issue doesn't meet project criteria, wontfix when the project won't address it, duplicate when another issue already covers it, accessibility for accessibility-related work, and needs triage for items not yet reviewed by a maintainer.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That list already teaches a vocabulary for participation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. A consistent label vocabulary makes collaboration faster.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you get to the Labels page in a repository?

speaker: ALEX
text: From the repository, go to the Issues tab, then find the Labels link or button in the toolbar near Milestones. The Labels page lists every label with its color, name, and description. For Windows screen reader users with NVDA or JAWS, you can navigate to the Issues tab, press K to move through links until you find the Labels link, then press Enter. For VoiceOver on macOS, go to the Issues tab, use Quick Nav K to find the Labels link, and activate it with VO+Space.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there's also a terminal option.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. With GitHub CLI, you can list all labels with descriptions using gh label list. If you want structured output, you can use gh label list with JSON fields such as name and description.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That can be especially helpful if someone prefers terminal output they can pipe, search, or reformat.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's actually apply labels. How does that work on an issue or PR?

speaker: ALEX
text: In the browser on GitHub.com, open the issue or pull request. In the right sidebar, find Labels and activate the gear or edit button. A dropdown opens with available labels. Select one or more labels, then click outside or press Escape. The selections save automatically.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And that same idea applies to pull requests, not just issues?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Labels can be applied to both.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about other tools?

speaker: ALEX
text: In github.dev, label management isn't available in the code editor itself, so you use the issue or PR page. In VS Code Desktop with the GitHub Pull Requests extension, you can open the issue in the GitHub sidebar and use the label area to add or remove labels. In GitHub Desktop, labels aren't directly supported, so you'd use View on GitHub in the browser.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And on the command line?

speaker: ALEX
text: With GitHub CLI, you can edit an issue and add a label, or add multiple labels at once by passing a comma-separated list. You can do the same for a pull request.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's make the accessibility steps concrete.

speaker: ALEX
text: For NVDA or JAWS, navigate to the Labels heading in the sidebar, activate the Labels button, use arrow keys in the dropdown, press Enter to select or deselect a label, and type to filter, like typing access to jump toward accessibility. Press Escape to close. For VoiceOver, find the Labels heading, activate the button with VO+Space, navigate the dropdown with VO+Down or arrow keys, select with VO+Space, and use typing to filter.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the key actions are open the control, navigate, select, filter if needed, and close.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's name the common tasks explicitly: add a label to an issue, add multiple labels at once, remove a label, and add a label to a PR.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Those are the core operations learners should expect.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Once labels exist, filtering is where they really become useful.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's right. You can filter issues by label to narrow a long list. For example, show only issues with a specific label like accessibility. You can also combine multiple labels to find issues that meet several conditions at once.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Like issues that are both documentation and good first issue?

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. You can also combine label filters with state, such as open issues with a particular label. And you can search across labels more generally when you're exploring patterns in the repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So labels aren't just tags. They're handles for finding work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Well said.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What if the label you need doesn't exist yet?

speaker: ALEX
text: Then you create a new label, assuming you have permission. On the Labels page, there's an option to create a label by giving it a name, description, and color. You can create a basic new label, or create one with a specific color to support a consistent visual system.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Does the description matter much?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The description is especially important for accessibility and clarity. A good label description tells contributors when the label should be used. That's useful for everyone and especially helpful on the Labels page where screen readers announce both name and description.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Any learning summary for labels?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Think of labels as reusable categories. Keep them meaningful. Use a stable vocabulary. Apply only what helps. And remember that labels support both quick scanning and powerful filtering.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Next up: milestones. How are milestones different from labels?

speaker: ALEX
text: A milestone groups issues and pull requests toward a shared target, often a release, phase, or deadline. Labels answer, "What kind of work is this?" Milestones answer, "What larger goal or time-bound effort is this part of?"

speaker: JAMIE
text: So labels classify, milestones group.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you navigate to milestones?

speaker: ALEX
text: From the Issues area of a repository, find the Milestones link near Labels. The milestones page lists available milestones. When you open one, you're reading a summary of that target: its title, optional description, due date if there is one, and progress based on open and closed items.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That progress view is helpful. It's not just a bucket; it's a tracking tool.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Reading a milestone means noticing what it represents and how much work remains.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you open a milestone from an issue workflow?

speaker: ALEX
text: You can go directly from the Milestones page and activate the milestone title. Or from an issue, if a milestone is already assigned, you can follow that reference to inspect the milestone's details.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And how do you add an issue to a milestone?

speaker: ALEX
text: Open the issue, go to the right sidebar, find the Milestone control, and assign one. If you're using GitHub CLI, you can edit the issue and set or change the milestone there as well.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the chapter specifically expects learners to understand three milestone operations: assign an issue to a milestone, remove it from a milestone, and list issues in a milestone.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Assigning places the issue into that tracked target. Removing detaches it if it was misclassified or deprioritized. Listing issues in a milestone lets you review everything contributing to that goal.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about creating a milestone?

speaker: ALEX
text: If you have permission, go to the Milestones page and create one with a title, description, and optionally a due date. A good milestone name is specific enough to communicate a concrete target, like a release, a sprint, or a hackathon phase.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What's the main learning card for milestones?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use milestones for grouped outcomes, not tiny categories. If labels are descriptive tags, milestones are shared destinations.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now cross-references. This is one of those GitHub features people use constantly without always naming it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Very true. A cross-reference is when one GitHub item mentions another in a way GitHub understands and links. Common types include referencing an issue from another issue, referencing an issue from a pull request, and using special closing keywords in a pull request to connect code changes to issues.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's start with the basic typing pattern.

speaker: ALEX
text: The most common form is the number reference, like hash 42, inside the same repository. GitHub turns that into a link to issue or pull request number 42. You can also reference items across repositories using owner slash repo, then hash number.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And when you do that, GitHub often creates a visible backlink, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. That's one of the most useful effects. The referenced issue often shows that it was mentioned elsewhere, which helps people trace related work and discussion.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now the special case: the closes keyword.

speaker: ALEX
text: Important one. If a pull request description or a merged commit message uses a closing keyword like closes, fixes, or resolves followed by an issue reference, GitHub can automatically close the linked issue when the pull request is merged into the right branch.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So not when the PR is opened.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. The keyword fires on merge, not merely on mention. That's crucial. A plain reference links discussion. A closing keyword links discussion plus intended completion.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a subtle but important distinction.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. And it's part of writing responsibly on GitHub. If you're not sure the PR fully solves the issue, use a plain reference rather than a closing keyword.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What's the compact learning summary here?

speaker: ALEX
text: Cross-references connect context. Plain references say, "these items are related." Closing keywords say, "merging this should complete that issue." Use them intentionally.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's talk about GitHub Projects. People sometimes confuse projects with milestones.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's common. A GitHub Project is a broader planning and tracking space. It can include issues, pull requests, and draft items, arranged in views like tables or boards. Projects are useful for workflow management across a repository, or even across multiple repositories.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if milestones are targets, projects are like planning dashboards.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you find a project?

speaker: ALEX
text: You may find one from a repository, an organization, or a user profile, depending on how it's set up. Look for a Projects tab or project links associated with the repository or organization.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Once inside a project, what does table view look like conceptually?

speaker: ALEX
text: Table view presents each item as a row with fields in columns. Those fields might include title, status, assignee, labels, milestone, repository, and other custom properties.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for screen reader users, what matters per row?

speaker: ALEX
text: What is announced per row is usually the item title plus the current values for the visible fields. So you want to understand that each row is not just the issue title; it's a bundle of metadata. Moving row by row lets you hear status, owner, and category in sequence.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a great mental model.

speaker: ALEX
text: In board view, items are grouped into columns, often by status. Think of columns like To Do, In Progress, and Done or Needs Triage. You move across columns to understand workflow stage, and within a column to review the items at that stage.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So table view is dense metadata. Board view is stage-oriented.

speaker: ALEX
text: Precisely.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you add an issue to a project?

speaker: ALEX
text: There are a few common ways. From the issue page, use the sidebar control to add the issue to a project. Or within the project itself, add an existing item by selecting the issue. Once added, the project can track fields like status and priority separately from the issue's own labels and milestone.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That "separately" matters. Projects don't replace labels and milestones.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. They complement them. Labels describe. Milestones group toward goals. Projects manage workflow and planning.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the learning summary for projects?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use projects when you need a live workspace for planning, prioritizing, and moving work through stages.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's make this practical. The chapter includes an organization strategy for a hackathon.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. This is where all the pieces come together. For a hackathon or workshop repo, create a small, purposeful set of labels. For example, documentation, bug, accessibility, enhancement, good first issue, and needs triage. You might also include help wanted if community pickup is encouraged.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So not dozens of labels just because you can.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Keep the set teachable and useful.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What milestone should be created?

speaker: ALEX
text: A milestone representing the hackathon target. That might be the event name, a release goal, or a chapter completion target. The key is that all related issues can be grouped under that shared outcome.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And what's the workflow?

speaker: ALEX
text: New issues start in Needs Triage. After review, apply one to three meaningful labels. If the work belongs to the event goal, assign the milestone. Add it to the project and place it in the appropriate column, such as To Do or In Progress. As implementation proceeds, use cross-references to link issues and pull requests. If a pull request truly completes an issue, use a closing keyword so the issue closes on merge.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That sounds like a clean repeatable process.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. And it's scalable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There's also a hands-on prompt here: label and link.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The idea is to practice both organization and connection. Pick an issue, apply or recommend an appropriate label, and create a useful cross-reference to related work. That reinforces that GitHub organization is not just tagging; it's building navigable context.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we wrap, I want to return to one line from earlier about agents waking up when labels or states change.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's an excellent detail. Labels and issue states aren't only for humans. They're also signals for automation and agentic workflows. In a future capstone, you might design an agent that activates only when an issue gets a label like needs-review or accessibility-check. So good triage can trigger the right follow-up work automatically.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Which means classification can become action.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can you give us a final compact comparison of the four tools?

speaker: ALEX
text: Sure. Labels tell you what kind of work something is. Milestones tell you what larger target it's part of. Projects tell you where it sits in a workflow or planning system. Cross-references tell you what other conversations or changes it's connected to.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the habit behind all of them?

speaker: ALEX
text: Inspect carefully, classify consistently, explain your reasoning, and leave the repository easier to navigate than you found it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a strong open-source habit.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. And one final administrative note: the chapter includes authoritative sources and a section-level source map pointing back to GitHub Docs, the GitHub changelog, and the GitHub Projects, labels, and milestones documentation. Those references are there so you can verify details and keep learning from the primary sources.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if you finish this lesson, your next move is simple: open an issue, read it carefully, recommend or apply a label, consider the milestone and project placement, and connect it to related work if needed.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Clear triage is real contribution.
